title: Market Brief — Friday, March 27, 2026
summary: The most important signal in today's data is the divergence between H100 and H200 spot pricing: H100 dropped 4.3% day-over-day to $1.47/hr while H200 climbed 5.3% to $2.45/hr, a simultaneous move in opposite directions that signals a hardware generation rotation underway. H100 availability at 20% and H200 at 18% both remain supply-constrained below the 25% threshold, yet the market is repricing H100s downward even under scarcity, implying that demand is migrating to newer silicon. Meanwhile the LLMflation Index sits at 21.41, meaning the blended cost of frontier intelligence has collapsed 78.6% from the GPT-4 launch baseline of 100, with DeepSeek and open-source components effectively floored at 0.38 each — a 99.6% decline from the original benchmark.
paragraphs: The most important signal in today's data is the divergence between H100 and H200 spot pricing: H100 dropped 4.3% day-over-day to $1.47/hr while H200 climbed 5.3% to $2.45/hr, a simultaneous move in opposite directions that signals a hardware generation rotation underway. H100 availability at 20% and H200 at 18% both remain supply-constrained below the 25% threshold, yet the market is repricing H100s downward even under scarcity, implying that demand is migrating to newer silicon. Meanwhile the LLMflation Index sits at 21.41, meaning the blended cost of frontier intelligence has collapsed 78.6% from the GPT-4 launch baseline of 100, with DeepSeek and open-source components effectively floored at 0.38 each — a 99.6% decline from the original benchmark.

Token pricing reveals a market that has stratified into four distinct bands. At the ultra-premium tier, OpenAI's o3 Pro at $80/M output stands alone, priced 210x above DeepSeek V3.2's $0.38/M output — a spread so extreme it can only be justified by agentic or reasoning workloads where no substitute exists. Anthropic's Claude Opus 4.6 at $25/M output and GPT-5.4 at $15/M output occupy the premium tier. In the mid-range, Google's Gemini 2.5 Pro at $10/M output and OpenAI's GPT-5 at $10/M output are price-matched, suggesting active competitive benchmarking. At the floor, DeepSeek V3.2 at $0.38/M output and Meta's Llama 4 Maverick at $0.60/M output are priced below the $4.29/M production cost on H100 for a Llama 70B reference workload — meaning either they are running far more efficient architectures, smaller effective parameter counts, or they are subsidizing every token served. xAI's Grok 4.1 Fast at $0.50/M output falls into the same sub-cost band, joining the cash-burn club.

The GPU compute market is uniformly supply-constrained across all three hardware generations. H100 availability is 20% with 49 of 250 GPUs available, H200 is at 18% with 63 of 350 GPUs, and B200 is at 20% with 58 of 295 GPUs — all below the 25% constraint threshold. B200 spot median pricing at $7.81/hr with a range of $3.13 to $9.00/hr shows the widest spread of any chip, a 187% gap between floor and ceiling that signals an immature and illiquid market still in early deployment. The H100 price decline to $1.47/hr against tightening availability suggests either contract roll-offs or demand substitution toward H200 and B200, while the H200 price increase to $2.45/hr confirms active demand migration to next-gen hardware.

Inference margins on the Llama 70B reference workload are negative at the medium pricing tier across all hardware: -43% on H100, -57% on H200, and -90% on B200, meaning any provider selling inference at mid-market rates on B200 hardware is losing $0.90 for every dollar of compute consumed. Profitability only materializes at the high tier on H100 at 28% margin and H200 at 22%, while B200 barely breaks even at 5%. Premium and ultra tiers generate 87-97% margins across all hardware, but these tiers correspond to the $10-80/M output price band occupied exclusively by OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google's flagship models. The implication is stark: the bottom half of the pricing table — DeepSeek, Meta, xAI, and even OpenAI's Mini models — cannot be profitably served on current spot GPU pricing without either proprietary silicon, aggressive quantization, or deliberate subsidy.

OpenAI holds the widest pricing portfolio in the market, spanning from $2/M output on GPT-5 Mini to $80/M on o3 Pro, giving it pricing power at every tier and the ability to cross-subsidize aggressively — grade A for strategic positioning but the $80/M o3 Pro price is a margin extraction play that will face pressure as reasoning models commoditize. Anthropic is the most expensive per-tier provider, with Claude Opus 4.6 at $25/M output and Sonnet 4.6 at $15/M output mapping to the LLMflation component score of 25, the highest of any provider — grade B-minus, as premium pricing without demonstrated margin superiority is a vulnerability. Google's Gemini 2.5 Flash at $2.50/M output and Pro at $10/M output reflect a disciplined two-tier strategy with the lowest LLMflation component at 10, grade B-plus for cost leadership at the frontier. DeepSeek is running V3.2 at $0.38/M output against a reference production cost of $4.29/M, implying a subsidy of roughly $3.91 per million tokens; at any meaningful volume this is a strategic loss leader likely backstopped by state-adjacent capital — grade C for sustainability, A for disruption. Meta's Llama 4 Maverick at $0.60/M output is essentially an ecosystem play designed to drive adoption rather than generate inference revenue, grade B for strategy but irrelevant to margin analysis. xAI's Grok 4.1 Fast at $0.50/M output is priced to buy market share in a crowded low-end tier with no visible moat — grade C-minus.

A portfolio manager looking at this data today should overweight exposure to GPU infrastructure providers benefiting from the triple supply constraint across H100, H200, and B200, where all availability rates sit at 18-20% — physical scarcity is the one signal that is unambiguous. On the inference layer, the margin data says only premium-tier API providers are generating positive economics, which means long OpenAI and cautiously long Google, while treating Anthropic as a show-me story at its elevated price points. Short or avoid pure-play inference at the commodity tier where DeepSeek and xAI are pricing below production cost — that is a subsidy war with no visible end and no margin of safety. The 78.6% collapse in the LLMflation Index over three years is structurally deflationary for any business model built on token revenue alone, so the investable thesis is in the picks-and-shovels layer and in the application layer that benefits from cheaper intelligence, not in the inference middlemen.
bullets: 21.41 LLMflation Index represents a 78.6% decline from the base 100 at GPT-4 launch, with DeepSeek and open-source components at 0.38 each reflecting a 99.6% cost collapse at the low end.

210x pricing spread exists between the most expensive model (o3 Pro at $80/M output) and the cheapest (DeepSeek V3.2 at $0.38/M output), the widest dispersion ever observed in a single inference market.
$3.91/M token implied subsidy on DeepSeek V3.2, calculated as the gap between its $0.38/M sell price and the $4.29/M H100 reference production cost for a 70B-class model.
H200 spot price rose 5.3% day-over-day from $2.33 to $2.45/hr while H100 fell 4.3% from $1.54 to $1.47/hr, a divergence signaling active demand migration to newer hardware.
18% H200 availability is the tightest of any GPU class, with only 63 of 350 tracked GPUs available, indicating the most acute supply constraint in the current market.
B200 spot price range of $3.13 to $9.00/hr represents a 187% spread between floor and ceiling, the widest of any GPU class and a marker of an illiquid and still-maturing market.
Negative 90% margin on B200 hardware at medium-tier pricing means providers lose $0.90 on every dollar of inference revenue at mid-market rates on the newest silicon.
Anthropic carries the highest LLMflation component score at 25 out of a blended index of 21.41, making it the single most expensive provider relative to the market composite.
OpenAI's GPT-5 and Google's Gemini 2.5 Pro are exact price-matched at $1.25/M input and $10/M output, evidence of direct competitive benchmarking at the frontier tier.
97% margin at the ultra tier on both H100 and H200 hardware confirms that premium reasoning and agentic models remain extraordinarily profitable for providers who can command top-of-market pricing.
